segments.s732-g1: چنین تا بیامد مه فرودین is a temporal clause: چنین 'thus' + تا 'when/until' introduces the time; بیامد is a past tense verb 'came/arrived' (3rd singular). مه فرودین is a month name (مه = ماه, 'month' here + فرودین 'Farvardin'), so the whole segment means 'thus, when the month Farvardin arrived.'
segments.s732-g2: بیاراست گلبرگ روی زمین places the verb before its object in poetic word order: بیاراست (past 'adorned') governs گلبرگ (the thing adorning, 'petals'), and روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on the surface/face of the earth.' The verb is simple past and ties back to the time clause.
segments.s732-g3: This clause is Subject — source — result: جهان 'the world' is subject, از نم ابر 'from the moisture of the cloud(s)' gives the source/causal phrase, and پر ژاله شد is the predicate with پر 'full (of)' + ژاله 'dew' and شد 'became' (past). The verb شد is the simple past copula meaning 'became.'
segments.s732-g4: همه کوه وهامون is a compound subject 'all the mountains and Hamun' (وهامون = 'and Hamun' / the Hamun plain); پراز لاله شد is the predicate with پراز 'full of' (poetic variant of پر از) + لاله 'tulips' and شد 'became.' Word order is subject before predicate with شد as the past intransitive/copulative verb.
segments.s732-g5: بزرگان 'the great ones' is the plural subject; به بازی 'toward play' expresses purpose and به باغ 'to the garden' gives the destination; آمدند is the past plural verb 'they came.' Poetic sequence places purpose and location before the verb.
segments.s732-g6: همه میش و آهو is a plural nominal list 'all the ewes and gazelles' and به راغ is a prepositional phrase 'to/into راغ' (here a poetic place-image 'place of longing/desire'); آمدند is again past plural 'they came.' The clause parallels the previous line in structure.
segments.s732-g7: چو here means 'when'; خسرو is the subject (Khosrow/the king); گشاده در باغ is a descriptive phrase 'wide/open in the garden' where گشاده 'open/spacious' qualifies باغ 'garden'; دید is the past verb 'saw.' The clause is a temporal 'when' clause introducing what he perceived.
segments.s732-g8: همه چشمه باغ is a genitive-noun phrase 'all the springs of the garden'; پر ماغ is the predicate 'full of mages/magi' (پر = full of, ماغ here used poetically as 'magi'); دید is the past verb 'he saw.' The verb applies to the whole preceding nominal phrase.
segments.s732-g41: بکشتند is a past transitive verb in third‑person plural (‘they killed’). او را shows the object (او = 'him/her', را = definite object marker) so the clause means ‘they killed him’. به here functions like English ‘with/by’ to introduce the manner (‘with lamentation and pain’). زاری و درد are nouns in a prepositional complement ('lamentation and sorrow/pain').
segments.s732-g42: کجا normally means ‘where’, but here it is used idiomatically/exclamatorily as ‘how’ or ‘in what state’. The repeated بد + اندیش forms a descriptive compound: بد (first) is the adjective ‘bad/evil’ and اندیش is a deverbal/agentive stem meaning ‘thinker’ or ‘one who thinks’ → ‘evil‑minded’. The second بد can be read as an adverbial/intensifying use ‘ill/poorly’ modifying اندیش; و links to the next adjective بیکار ‘idle’, and مرد is the noun ‘man’. Overall the phrase laments the miserable death of an evil‑minded, idle man.
segments.s732-g43: هممی (an abstract noun ‘aspiration/effort’) begins the line as a topic; هر زمانش ‘at every moment’ is a time adverbial modifying the predicate. فزون بود is a predicative construction (‘was increasing/was abundant’) in past tense (بود = ‘was’). بخت (placed at the end for poetic word order) is the noun ‘fortune’ and functions as the subject/theme whose state is described: ‘fortune was increasing’ or ‘his fortune grew’.
segments.s732-g44: ازان = ‘from that’ or ‘of that’ introduces origin or source. تاجور is a noun ‘merchant/trader’; خسروانی is an adjectival/attributive form ‘royal’ (of the king) modifying تاجور. درخت is the noun ‘tree’ (here likely metaphorical). The phrase is elliptical/poetic: ‘from that royal merchant (came) a tree’ or ‘from that royal merchant’s stock/line sprang a tree’, with some words omitted for meter.
segments.s732-g33: همی is a narrative/adverbial particle marking continuous or habitual action ('keeps/was about to'); the phrase follows classical word order where the verb کند ('he does/makes') appears at the end and governs the clause. گربه is the patient/object here ('the cat'), and از خانه بیرون is a prepositional phrase 'out of the house' (از + خانه + بیرون). No object marker را is used; کند is a third-person present/ narrative verb form used in epic diction.
segments.s732-g34: دگر is an adverb here meaning 'again/then' (indicating the next action). ناودان is the object 'gutter/water‑spout'; یک به یک is the idiom 'one by one' where به functions as 'by' linking the numerals. بشکند is the third‑person present/narrative form (ب‑ + root) meaning 'breaks'/'he breaks' (classical present used in narrative).
segments.s732-g35: بخندید is a past/narrative verb 'laughed' but here takes the sense 'laughed at' because it is followed by ز گفتار زن ('at/by the woman's speech'); the verb precedes the subject (خسرو) as is common in poetry. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/at' that introduces the cause or target of the laughter.
segments.s732-g36: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him/to her' ('to her' here) marking the indirect object. گفت is the past of 'say' (here 'said to her'); کای is the vocative particle 'O' used to address someone, and ماه لشکرشکن is a poetic epithet 'moon, army‑breaker' (ماه = beautiful one; لشکرشکن = one who breaks armies). The whole is direct address: he said to her, 'O moon, army‑breaker.'
segments.s732-g37: ز ری is the prepositional phrase 'from Rey' (ز = from). باز here is an adverb 'back/again' and combines with خوان (the verb stem 'call') to mean 'call back' — a poetic compound where خوان is the verbal root. آن بد اندیش را is the definite object ('that ill‑thinking man') marked by را. The verb (implied or in the surrounding clause) is the action 'called back'.
segments.s732-g38: چو is a simile 'like/as'; آهرمن names Ahriman (the devil) used for comparison. آن مرد بد کیش را is another definite object phrase 'that man of bad/evil creed' (کیش = creed/sect), again marked by را; the clause echoes or apposes the previous object, saying he is like Ahriman. The verb is omitted here by ellipsis (understood from the previous clause).
segments.s732-g39: فرستاد is the past tense 'he sent'. کس زشت رخ is a noun phrase 'an ugly‑faced person' (کس = someone; زشت رخ = ugly‑faced). رابخواند is a verbal compound (را + خواند) meaning 'to call back / to summon' — here it expresses the purpose of the sending: 'sent (someone) to summon him.'
segments.s732-g40: همان is a demonstrative/adverb 'that very' or 'the same'. خشم بهرام is a genitive phrase 'the anger/fury of Bahram' (بهرام = proper name). با او براند literally places با او ('with him' / marking the person affected) before the verb براند 'drove away' (براند is third person present/narrative); together it means 'that very fury of Bahram drove him off.'
segments.s732-g9: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 3rd‑person verb meaning “he commanded/said.” تا here introduces a subordinate temporal/event clause meaning “when/as soon as” (classical usage). دردمیدند is 3rd‑person plural past meaning “they lamented/blew in lament” (here indicating the mournful sounding); بوق is the direct object meaning “horn/trumpet.” Together this clause = “he commanded that when they lamented and blew the horn…”.
segments.s732-g10: بیاورد is a present‑subjunctive/imperative subordinate form here (reporting command) meaning “(they) should bring” or “bring.” پس is an adverb meaning “then/afterwards.” جامهای is the plural genitive/construct form “cups.” خلوق is an attributive adjective/noun used here to qualify the cups (poetically “of the courteous/good‑mannered”); the whole clause says “bring then the cups of the courteous.”
segments.s732-g11: نشستند is a 3rd‑person plural past verb meaning “they sat down.” (Note: the provided lemma/senses list for this token does not include ‘sit’; therefore the sense index below is -1.) بر سبزه is a prepositional phrase: بر = “on/upon” and سبزه = “green growth/grass.” می‌خواستند is imperfective/past habitual 3rd‑person plural meaning “they wished/intended.” Together: “They sat upon the grass; they intended/wished …”.
segments.s732-g12: به introduces a benefactive/purpose sense “for/to.” شادی is the noun “joy/celebration.” زبان is used here as “speech/utterance” (not merely the physical tongue). را is the Persian definite object marker attaching to زبان to mark it as the direct object. بیاراستند is past 3rd‑person plural perfective meaning “they adorned/embellished.” The clause means “they (wished) to adorn their speech for joy.”
segments.s732-g13: بیاورد is the reported/imperative‑style form meaning “(he) brought” or “(let) bring.” پس functions adverbially as “then/afterwards.” گردیه is a participial/adjectival form from گردیدن meaning “turned/curled” (here describing the kitten’s posture or shape). گربکی is a diminutive noun meaning “little cat/kitten.” The phrase reads “then he brought a little curled kitten.”
segments.s732-g14: که is a relative pronoun “which/that.” پیدا here means “visible/seen.” نبد is an old/stylistic negative past form meaning “was not / did not exist.” گربه is “cat.” از is the preposition “from/since.” کودکی is the noun “childhood / kittenhood.” The relative clause literally: “that had not been seen as a cat since (its) childhood.”
segments.s732-g15: بر is the preposition “upon/on.” اسپی (classical form of اسپ) means “steed/horse.” نشانده is a past participle/causative meaning “placed/caused to sit.” ستامی is an adjective/noun meaning “oppressive/oppressor” (here describing the person placed on the horse). بزر is an adjective meaning “great/mighty.” Together: “He set/placed a great oppressive one upon a horse.”
segments.s732-g16: به here has a locative/into sense “in/within.” زر = “gold.” اندرون = “interior/within.” چند is an indefinite numeral/adjective “several/some.” گونه = “kinds/types.” گهر = “gem/jewel.” The noun cluster describes contents: “within the golden interior were several kinds of jewels.” (Word order is poetic: adjectives and genitives cluster after the locative phrase.)
segments.s732-g25: ابا گردیه گفت is a compact, slightly archaic phrase: ابا here carries the old sense of reluctance/refusal and گردیه is a verbal/adjectival form 'having turned' — together 'turning away reluctantly'. گفت is the past verb 'he said'. کز آرزوی uses کز (relative/connecting 'from/ concerning which') + آرزوی 'desire/longing', introducing the content of the reported speech ('from what longing...'). Word order reflects classical Persian compactness where the circumstance ('turning away reluctantly') precedes the main verb 'said'.
segments.s732-g26: چه باید بگو‌ای زن خوب روی is a direct question inside the reported speech. چه 'what' + باید 'ought/should' form a modal construction 'what should (I)'. بگو is the verb 'say' and ای is the vocative particle addressing the addressee. زن خوب روی is a noun phrase 'woman of good/beautiful face' (خوب used adjectivally with روی 'face' = 'fair‑faced' or 'beautiful'). In short: 'What should I say, O fair‑faced woman?'
segments.s732-g27: زن چاره‌گر is the noun phrase 'the resourceful/ingenious woman'. برد here functions as a past verbal action 'brought/took' (classical narrative past). پیشش is a locative/benefactive 'before him / in his presence' and نماز is the direct object 'prayer' or 'devotional act' — 'the clever woman brought a prayer before him'.
segments.s732-g28: بدو گفت is 'she said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'said'). What follows is a vocative: کای = 'O' (vocative particle), شاه 'king', گردن‌فراز an epithet 'neck‑raising' literally but used as 'proud' or 'lordly' — together addressing the ruler: 'She said to him, "O proud‑necked king".'
segments.s732-g29: بمن بخش ری را is an imperative/request construction where بمن 'to me' is the beneficiary, بخش (here) denotes the thing to be given — the district/portion — and ری را is the definite object 'the Rey (region)'. The order places the indirect object before verb and object (poetic flexibility). خرد یاد کن is an independent imperative clause: خرد 'wisdom' + یاد 'remembrance' + کن 'do' → 'remember wisdom' or 'keep wisdom in mind'.
segments.s732-g30: دل غمگنان از غم آزاد کن is an imperative: دل غمگنان 'the hearts of the sorrowful' (noun + genitive/possessive relation), از غم 'from sorrow', آزاد کن 'set free / free' (imperative). The verb آزاد کن governs the preceding phrases: 'Free the hearts of the sorrowful from sorrow.'
segments.s732-g31: ز ری مردک شوم رابازخوان is a compact, slightly inverted command: ز ری 'from Rey', مردک 'that wretch/little man', را (embedded as part of رابازخوان) is the object marker and بازخوان 'call back/fetch' is the verb. Poetic word order scatters the elements, but the sense is 'Call back/fetch that wretch from Rey.' (شوم appears as a potential/subjunctive element in form but is serving in the clause structure; the main action is the command to fetch.)
segments.s732-g32: ورا مرد بد کیش و بد ساز دان is an instruction to judge/consider: ورا 'him' (to/for him), مرد 'man', بد کیش 'of bad creed/bad character', و بد ساز 'and of evil devices/ill measures', دان is the verb 'know/consider' (imperative sense here: 'consider/let it be known'). So: 'Consider him a man of bad creed and evil devices.' The adjectives are joined by و and modify مرد.
segments.s732-g17: فروهشته is a poetic participle/adjective meaning 'hanging down' or 'cast down' and introduces the image; از گوش او is a prepositional source phrase 'from her ear'; گوشوار follows as the thing that hangs (earring). Word order places the participial descriptor before the source phrase and the noun being described, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s732-g18: This clause names the agent (نگار, 'the beauty') at the end in typical Persian inversion; کرده is the perfect participle 'had made/done' and governs what follows. به ناخن بر (به ... بر) functions as a locative phrase 'on the nail/tip', از لاله is a descriptive simile 'of/from the tulip' (i.e. tulip‑red), so the whole means 'the beauty had made (her) nails like tulips.' Note the omission of an explicit verb for 'painted' is compensated by کرده + descriptive phrase.
segments.s732-g19: This is a nominal descriptive cluster: بدیده 'bad‑faced / ill‑favoured' (here describing the look), followed by the adjective چوقار 'stately/proud'. و connects to the next prepositional phrase; به رخ چون بهار is a simile 'with a face like spring' where به رخ ('to/at the face') marks the domain being likened.
segments.s732-g20: چو (as/like) introduces a simile 'as/like a wine‑drinker' (می‌خواره). بد modifies چشم to give 'bad‑eyed' or 'bewitching eye'; او is the pronoun 'her'. پر خمار is a predicate: پر 'full (of)' + خمار 'intoxication' yields 'her eye was full of intoxication.' The clause uses verbless predication typical of Persian poetic description (predicate phrase following the nominal subject).
segments.s732-g21: همی‌تاخت is an imperfective/continuous past verb 'kept rushing/was darting'. چون کودکی is a simile 'like a child'; گرد باغ is a circumstantial phrase 'around the garden' (گرد = around). Word order is verb → simile → place, typical for vivid narrative action in classical Persian.
segments.s732-g22: This is a descriptive noun phrase continuing the ornament imagery: فروهشته again 'hanging/scattered', followed by the source phrase از باره زرین جناغ 'from a golden shower/burst on (her) breast/chest'. The sequence has an implied verb of hanging/scattering; باره here functions as 'shower/burst' (a poetic word for a scattering), and زرین جناغ is a genitive 'golden sternum/bosom.'
segments.s732-g23: لب شاه ایران is a genitival construction 'the lip of the Shah of Iran' (noun + possessor). پر از خنده شد is a predicate 'became full of laughter' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of', خنده = 'laughter', شد = 'became'). The verb شد gives the change of state.
segments.s732-g24: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; کهتران here means 'the lesser men/courtiers' (the provided lemma's senses don't match exactly). خنده را uses the object marker را to topicalize 'laughter', and بنده شد literally 'became servants/slaves (of it)'—a fixed idiom meaning 'were overcome by/enslaved to the laughter.' The clause uses passive/experiential sense of شد ('became').

sentences.s732-s1: Thus, when the month Farvardin arrived, petals adorned the face of the earth.
sentences.s732-s2: The world, from the moisture of clouds, became full of dew; all the mountains and Hamun became filled with tulips.
sentences.s732-s3: The great ones came to the garden to play; all the ewes and gazelles came to the place of longing.
sentences.s732-s4: When Khosrow saw the garden wide open, he saw all the garden's springs full of magi.
sentences.s732-s21: They killed him amid lamentation and sorrow; how miserably that evil‑minded and idle man died!
sentences.s732-s22: His aspiration was ever greater at every moment; fortune kept increasing — from that royal merchant came a tree.
sentences.s732-s17: He drove the cat out of the house, and then broke the gutters one by one.
sentences.s732-s18: Khosrow laughed at the woman's words, and said to her, 'O moon, breaker of armies.'
sentences.s732-s19: They called back from Rey that ill‑thinking man; like Ahriman, that man of evil creed.
sentences.s732-s20: He sent an ugly‑faced fellow to summon him back; that very fury of Bahram drove him away.
sentences.s732-s5: He commanded that, when they lamented and sounded the horn, they should then bring the cups for the courteous.
sentences.s732-s6: They sat upon the grass; they wished, for joy, to adorn their speech.
sentences.s732-s7: Then he brought a little curled kitten which had not been seen as a cat since childhood.
sentences.s732-s8: Upon a horse he had placed a mighty oppressor; within a golden interior were several kinds of jewels.
sentences.s732-s13: Turning away with reluctance, he said, "From what longing? What should I say, O fair‑faced woman?"
sentences.s732-s14: The resourceful woman brought a prayer before him and said to him, "O proud‑necked king,"
sentences.s732-s15: Grant me the district of Rey; remember wisdom; free the hearts of the sorrowful from sorrow.
sentences.s732-s16: Fetch that wretch from Rey; consider him a man of bad creed and of evil devices.
sentences.s732-s9: An earring hung from her ear; the beauty had painted her nails tulip-red.
sentences.s732-s10: With proud, dark-set eyes and a face like spring; like a wine‑drinker, her gaze was full of intoxication.
sentences.s732-s11: She darted about the garden like a child; from her golden bosom hung scattered ornaments.
sentences.s732-s12: The Shah of Iran's lips filled with laughter; all the lesser men were made servants of that laughter.
